title: if / else if / else
successMessage: 78 → C. Conditions execute top to bottom; the first true branch runs. Change score to 91 and it prints A. Always use braces — missing braces caused Apple's famous 'goto fail' SSL bug.

title: Logical operators: &&, ||, !
instruction:
Combine boolean conditions:

- `&&` — both must be true (AND)
- `||` — at least one must be true (OR)
- `!` — invert (NOT)

**Short-circuit**: `&&` stops at first `false`, `||` stops at first `true`.
successMessage: Short-circuit evaluation: if x=0, the second part of && never runs — no division by zero. This is why checking for null/zero FIRST is important in compound conditions.

title: Ternary operator
instruction:
One-line conditional expression:

```cpp
type result = condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse;
```

Perfect for simple assignments. Don't nest — it kills readability.
successMessage: Ternary for simple decisions in one line. Nesting ternaries is legal but cruel to readers — use if/else for anything complex.

title: switch — match exact values
instruction:
C++ `switch` works on `int`, `char`, and `enum` (not strings).

⚠️ **Always use `break`** — otherwise execution falls through to the next case.
successMessage: case 6 falls through to case 7 intentionally — both produce 'Weekend!'. Intentional fallthrough is the one time you omit break. Otherwise: never forget break or you get silent bugs.

title: Comparison operators
instruction:
C++ comparison operators:

- `==` equal, `!=` not equal
- `<`, `>`, `<=`, `>=`
- `<=>` (spaceship, C++20) — three-way comparison

⚠️ `if (x = 5)` is assignment, not comparison! The compiler often warns about this — enable warnings (`-Wall`).
successMessage: == for comparison, = for assignment. Accidentally writing = instead of == in a condition is one of the oldest C++ bugs — compilers warn about it with -Wall.

title: Build a grade calculator
instruction:
No starter code.

Given scores: `85, 92, 78, 96, 70`

1. Calculate the average
2. Assign a letter grade:
   - 90+ = A, 80+ = B, 70+ = C, 60+ = D, else F
3. Use the ternary operator for a pass/fail status (pass >= 70)
4. Print all three results
hint: double avg = (85 + 92 + 78 + 96 + 70) / 5.0; then if/else or nested ternary for grade.

codeChecks.message: Include the five scores in your calculation.
Use 5.0 (not 5) to avoid integer division in average.

successMessage: Average with letter grade and pass/fail. Division by 5.0 — not 5 — is critical. Chapter 5 done!
